text: Hello and welcome to this episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Kapil and with me I have Girish
from IOTA. Girish, welcome to the show. Thanks Kapil, it's good to be here. Glad to have you.
And Girish, can you tell us a little bit about IOTA? IOTA is an audience data marketplace with
a global footprint. We provide a privacy compliant way for advertisers to reach their target audiences
with precision and for data providers to monetize their data with transparency. And Girish, you
have built a new data platform on AWS. Can you tell us what were the challenges you were
trying to solve? The primary business problem we're trying to address is data quality. We
are certified by both the IAB Tech Lab and by Neutronion, the gold standards and ad tech data
quality certification. The way we achieve this quality is by using our machine learning algorithms
which are designed to optimize the trade-off between scale and data quality. In technical
terms it translates into a few different requirements. One is scalability given the volumes of data
we process, flexibility for our development teams, abstraction from the infrastructure layer,
and transparency on unit cost economics. So that all of this comes together to increase
delivery velocity. Great. And could you walk us through the diagram? I see a lot of moving
parts here. Sure. So this is really, you can think about it as a model life cycle and we articulate
that in code in the form of Airflow DAGs, PY Spark scripts, and SageMaker notebooks. We also have
our raw data, which comes to us from our supply side partners. And both of these we store in S3.
From S3, we use Airflow to pick up, to orchestrate our data pipelines. So this, when it goes into the top
over here, essentially what's going on is we're using Glue for pre-processing and feature selection.
That's where the PY Spark scripts come in. From there, the data flows into SageMaker for model training and
tuning. And also from here, data flows into SageMaker batch inference, which is where we run,
which we run on our held out data sets.
Right.
Down here on the inference side, again, Airflow is triggering a pipeline with Glue doing pre-processing.
And from there, the data is going into SageMaker for batch inference. Remember, this is an unlabeled data.
Mm-hmm.
So the generated labels are then stored in S3, from where it is distributed downstream to the rest of our pipeline.
Okay.
And in the middle over here, we have model selection and pipeline optimization. So both of these pipelines,
they push their performance and operational metrics into CloudWatch, from where we have an internal metrics
visualization tool, which we run on EC2, that helps our data science teams figure out what is the right model to push into
production for each use case. The selected models are pushed into S3, and that's what is used by the batch
inference job to run on the production workload. Our data science team also use the visualization to monitor
the production pipelines for things like model drift. At the same time, our data engineering and infrastructure teams,
they also use the metrics over here to ensure pipeline uptime and also look for optimization opportunities.
Right. And I can see there are clearly two different types of pipelines here. Can you tell us what's the typical
scale of data processed by these pipelines?
So up here for model training and tuning, we run a few hundreds of training jobs, each of which is processing
anywhere from a few hundred megs of data to a few hundred gigs of data. Down here on inference, we run a few
hundred inference jobs, each of which is processing terabyte-scale data. Ultimately, we are processing like
six to seven billion unique advertising IDs a month, mapping these into 70,000 different attributes. So all of this is running on a
ginormous distributed sparse matrix. Fantastic. And you are doing all this orchestration from Apache Airflow. Can you tell us why Airflow?
Primarily, it gives us a lot of flexibility for both our data science and data engineering teams. So typically, a data science team needs to work on very experimental features,

and they can do that on Airflow. But the data engineering team is optimizing production-scale performance, so they can do that as well on Airflow. So it's a single platform that both of them can use, and it's great for our velocity.
And what was your experience like integrating from Airflow with AWS services?
It was quite straightforward to orchestrate the services. It was mostly a question of following the docs. But then when we got into
final-grain configuration, we faced a bit of a learning curve. For example, we needed to figure out the right metrics to monitor here, so that we could
figure out how many DPOs to process for Glue, to configure for Glue. For SageMaker, we need to play around a bit with distributed learning and model averaging to get it to scale to our sizes of data.
Right. That's a lot of fantastic work done by your team. What would you say are the key outcomes of this project?
Team efficiency. I mean, right now we get most training jobs, training pipelines configured within three to five working days, which is half of what we used to do earlier.
And we get this with a lot more transparency on model performance, and also a lot more configurability across the board. So it's a huge win for the team.
That's fantastic. Girish, thanks a lot for sharing all these insights with our viewers. And thank you for watching this episode of This Is My Architecture.
